howItWorks.intro: Composting works best when the carbon-to-nitrogen (C:N) ratio is around 25:1 to 35:1, with 30:1 being the ideal target.
rules: Target C:N ratio for active composting: 25:1 to 35:1 (ideal: 30:1)
Green materials provide nitrogen (low C:N) — food scraps, grass, manure
Brown materials provide carbon (high C:N) — leaves, straw, cardboard
Blended C:N = (green_vol × green_CN + brown_vol × brown_CN) ÷ total_vol
Too much nitrogen (low ratio): smelly, slimy pile. Add browns.
Too much carbon (high ratio): slow decomposition. Add greens.
howItWorks.outro: In practice, a volumetric ratio of roughly 3 parts brown to 1 part green by volume achieves close to the 30:1 C:N target for most common materials.

faq.question: What is the ideal compost ratio?
faq.answer: The ideal carbon-to-nitrogen ratio is 25:1 to 35:1 by weight, with 30:1 as the sweet spot. By volume, this roughly translates to 3 parts brown materials to 1 part green materials, since browns are less dense.

faq.question: My compost smells bad — what should I do?
faq.answer: Bad smell means too much nitrogen (greens) or not enough air. Add brown materials like dry leaves, straw, or shredded cardboard, and turn the pile to add oxygen. The smell should improve within a day or two.

faq.question: My compost is not heating up — why?
faq.answer: Common causes: not enough nitrogen (add greens), pile is too small (needs 3×3×3 ft minimum), too dry (add water until damp like a wrung sponge), or needs turning for oxygen.

faq.question: How long does composting take?
faq.answer: Hot composting with proper C:N ratio and regular turning: 4-8 weeks. Cold composting (pile and wait): 6-12 months. Vermicomposting (worm bin): 3-6 months. The C:N ratio significantly affects speed.
